![Nature Research Intelligence](/research-intelligence/build/vectors/nature-research-logo.svg)

# Inertial Confinement Fusion Research

Inertial confinement fusion (ICF) research underpins the ongoing quest to replicate the nuclear fusion processes that power the stars within a controlled laboratory environment. By using intense bursts of energy – often delivered by high-power lasers – to compress and heat small capsules of fusion fuel, scientists are advancing our understanding of plasma physics and the conditions needed for thermonuclear ignition. This multidisciplinary field integrates experimental innovations, advanced computational modelling and precise diagnostic techniques to address challenges such as symmetry control, energy loss, and efficient coupling of energy to the target. The developments in ICF offer prospects not only for a potentially transformative clean energy source but also for applications in high-energy-density physics, national security and the study of astrophysical phenomena, positioning ICF research as a matter of global scientific and technological significance.

## Research from Nature Portfolio

Recent studies have demonstrated significant progress in addressing the challenges of achieving and maintaining a burning plasma state. New experimental campaigns at major facilities have resolved issues of mode symmetry by empirically correcting for asymmetries that previously degraded fusion performance [3]. In parallel, breakthrough experiments have successfully reached a burning-plasma regime where the α-particle self-heating effect dominates the energy balance, thereby confirming critical theoretical predictions regarding ignition-scale plasmas [4]. Complementing these efforts, statistical modelling applied to direct-drive configurations has recently achieved a tripling of fusion yield, offering a promising route towards higher energy output and improved energy gain factors [5]. Collectively, these Nature Portfolio contributions underscore a marked trend towards optimising target design and energy coupling, both of which are essential for the eventual commercialisation of fusion energy.

## Research from all publishers

Recent work in ICF research has focused on both experimental and computational initiatives designed to optimise fusion yields and enhance our understanding of plasma behaviour. For example, kinetic simulations have provided new insights into the role of large-angle collisions among deuterium–tritium ions, leading to the unexpected detection of supra-thermal ions during fusion burning plasmas [1]. Concurrently, investigations into the performance of accelerator systems, including emerging induction synchrotron approaches, suggest that alternative methodologies could supplement laser-based experiments by providing enhanced target compression techniques [2]. Together, these studies highlight an evolving landscape where improved diagnostic capabilities, numerical precision and the integration of diverse acceleration mechanisms are gradually overcoming longstanding obstacles in reaching self-sustaining fusion reactions.

## Technical Terms

**Burning plasma:** A plasma in which the energy produced by fusion reactions is sufficient to maintain its temperature, thereby sustaining the fusion process.

**Direct drive:** An approach in which laser energy is directly focused on a fusion target, compressing it without intermediary energy conversion steps.

**Indirect drive:** A method wherein laser light is converted into X-rays within a hohlraum, which then uniformly irradiates and compresses the fusion capsule.

## [First Light Fusion shifts focus from power to amplify its technology and revenue](https://www.ans.org/news/2025-03-25/article-6890/first-light-fusion-shifts-focus-from-power-to-amplify-its-technology-and-revenue/)

![](/file/19951/Sandia-1-1200x900 2x1.jpg)

First Light Fusion [announced](https://firstlightfusion.com/media/first-light-fusion-sets-a-new-record-for-the-highest-pressure-recorded-on-sandias-z-machine/) last week that it has set a new record for the highest quartz pressure achieved on Sandia National Laboratories’ [Z machine](https://www.sandia.gov/z-machine/) using its amplifier technology to achieve an output pressure of 3.67 terapascal (TPa)—roughly doubling the pressure the company reached in its first experiment on the machine one year ago.

![Sponsored Article Image](/file/20331/t/WMG Sponcon Fig 3.jpg)

A message from WMG, Inc.

WMG FLTRSTOR™ System Overview

[Learn More](https://www.ans.org/news/2025-06-02/article-7042/wmg-fltrstor-system-overview/)

## [What’s different about Pacific Fusion’s pulsed magnetic concept?](https://www.ans.org/news/article-6523/whats-different-about-pacific-fusions-pulsed-magnetic-concept/)

![](/file/17981/Picture3.jpg)

With more than 40 fusion development companies announcing plans and funding, it’s hard for a newcomer to stand out, but Pacific Fusion is giving it a try. The company, based in Fremont, Calif., was founded in summer 2023 and [emerged](https://www.pacificfusion.com/updates/founders-letter) from “stealth mode” last Friday with $900 million in committed funding from investors, a team that includes people directly involved in the successful ignition experiments at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s National Ignition Facility (NIF), and a technical paper that makes a case for a pulsed magnetic fusion approach to fusion energy.

## [Colorado State begins constructing laser lab as public-private research hub](https://www.ans.org/news/article-6519/colorado-state-begins-constructing-laser-lab-as-publicprivate-research-hub/)

![](/file/17968/LaserGroundbreakin101624.jpg)

In the foothills of the Rocky Mountains on the outskirts of Fort Collins, Colo.—home to Colorado State University—work [began](https://source.colostate.edu/work-to-start-on-powerful-new-laser-facility-funded-by-public-private-partnership/) this month on a new laser facility funded by a public-private partnership. The private portion is $150 million from Marvel Fusion, announced in [August 2023](https://source.colostate.edu/csu-marvel-fusion-groundbreaking-laser-facility/), while $12.5 million—the latest funding for CSU from the Department of Energy’s Office of Fusion Energy Sciences (FES)—will support the new facility as part of LaserNetUS, a laser research network operated by DOE-FES to provide access to laser facilities for multidisciplinary researchers from the United States and abroad.

## [General Atomics R&D team recognized for contributions to NIF’s fusion ignition](https://www.ans.org/news/article-6288/general-atomics-rd-team-recognized-for-contributions-to-nifs-fusion-ignition/)

![](/file/16967/ga-inertial-fusion-rdteam-wins-coveted-award.jpg)

The National Ignition Facility (NIF) at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory has achieved fusion ignition at least five times, each time by directing its 192 high-powered lasers on a capsule containing a tiny, 2-millimeter target filled with hydrogen fuel. Not every shot achieves ignition, however. Tiny imperfections in the targets can mean fizzle, not fusion. But each of the targets used in successful experiments to date have something in common: they were characterized and selected by the 4Pi Integrated Metrology System, a new measurement system developed by General Atomics. Now, the team behind that system is being recognized.

GA [announced](https://www.ga.com/ga-inertial-fusion-r-d-team-wins-coveted-award) last week that its Metrology Research and Development team had won the 2024 "Team of the Year" R&D 100 Professional Award from *R&D World*. The magazine that each year announces the [R&D 100 awards](https://www.rdworldonline.com/explore-the-2024-rd-100-award-winners-and-finalists-the-oscars-of-innovation-at-your-fingertips/) that have been dubbed the “Oscars of Innovation” also selects just one “Team of the Year” and announces that award together with four other [professional awards](https://www.rdworldonline.com/rd-100-professional-awards-2024-winners/).

## [What’s happening in big fusion? A global update](https://www.ans.org/news/article-5588/whats-happening-in-big-fusion-a-global-update/)

![](/file/14105/Fusion Status Collage.jpg)

One year ago today, researchers at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory achieved a record shot at the National Ignition Facility (NIF) that set the world talking about the potential of fusion energy. And the buzz hasn’t stopped. Fusion energy is getting its most significant attention yet on the world stage at COP28 in Dubai, UAE, where John Kerry, U.S. special presidential envoy for climate, delivered a keynote address today titled “An inclusive fusion energy future,” followed by a panel discussion.

## [Focused Energy partners with LLNL on inertial fusion energy targets](https://www.ans.org/news/article-5526/focused-energy-partners-with-llnl-on-inertial-fusion-energy-targets/)

![](/file/13802/Picture1.png)

Focused Energy and Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory have signed a strategic partnership project agreement that will allow LLNL—home of the National Ignition Facility (NIF)—to help the company develop and assess isochoric compression target designs for inertial fusion energy. Focused Energy [announced the news](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20231107594701/en) on November 7.

## [Focused Energy and Xcimer Energy: DOE’s inertial fusion pilot picks](https://www.ans.org/news/article-5120/focused-energy-and-xcimer-energy-does-inertial-fusion-pilot-picks/)

![](/file/11927/Fusion Logos Focused Xcimer.jpg)

Focused Energy and Xcimer Energy are two of the eight fusion developers the Department of Energy selected in [late May](https://www.ans.org/news/article-5062/doe-picks-eight-fusion-companies-to-design-pilot-power-plants/) for funding under the public-private Milestone-Based Fusion Development Program, and the only developers using an inertial confinement concept. The Milestone program, announced in September 2022, was open to any fusion developer willing to undergo a competitive merit-review process, regardless of fusion confinement concept. But the prospects for inertial fusion concepts might have gotten a boost from Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s December 2022 achievement of scientific breakeven at the National Ignition Facility (NIF), which was announced shortly before the application window for the Milestone program closed.

## [DOE celebrates NIF ignition by funding R&D hubs for inertial fusion energy](https://www.ans.org/news/article-4994/doe-celebrates-nif-ignition-by-funding-rd-hubs-for-inertial-fusion-energy/)

![](/file/11322/Picture1.jpg)

Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory hosted current and former staff, government officials, and media on May 8 to celebrate the lab’s achievement of fusion ignition at the National Ignition Facility (NIF) on December 5, 2022. Energy secretary Jennifer Granholm and undersecretary for nuclear security and National Nuclear Security Administration administrator Jill Hruby were in attendance, and Granholm took the opportunity to announce [funding of up to $45 million](https://www.energy.gov/science/articles/department-energy-announces-45-million-inertial-fusion-energy-ife) to support inertial fusion energy (IFE) research and development. The Department of Energy’s Office of Science (DOE-SC) wants to establish multiple IFE Science and Technology Innovation Hubs (IFE S&T hubs), with total funding for 2023 of up to $9 million for projects lasting up to four years in duration.

## [First Light Fusion wants to operate a net gain inertial fusion machine in 2027](https://www.ans.org/news/article-4694/first-light-fusion-wants-to-operate-a-net-gain-inertial-fusion-machine-in-2027/)

![](/file/9857/image1-18.png)

Ignition and net gain at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s National Ignition Facility (NIF) in December 2022 focused global attention on the prospects of inertial fusion energy (IFE). First Light Fusion and the U.K. Atomic Energy Authority (UKAEA) acknowledged the achievement as they [announced plans on January 25](https://ccfe.ukaea.uk/first-light-fusion-to-build-demonstration-facility-at-ukaeas-culham-campus/) to design and build a demonstration facility known as Machine 4 at UKAEA’s Culham Campus in Oxford, U.K., using First Light’s “projectile approach” to IFE. Construction is expected to begin in 2024, and operations are “likely to commence” in 2027.

## [Breakeven breakthrough at the National Ignition Facility](https://www.ans.org/news/article-4575/breakeven-breakthrough-at-the-national-ignition-facility/)

![](/file/9590/Picture1.jpg)

It’s official: Early in the morning on December 5 at [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s](https://www.llnl.gov/) [National Ignition Facility](https://lasers.llnl.gov/) (NIF), the laser-triggered implosion of a meticulously engineered capsule of deuterium and tritium about the size of a peppercorn yielded, for the first time on Earth, more energy from a fusion reaction than was delivered to the capsule. The input of 2.05 megajoules (MJ) to the target heated the diamond-shelled, spherical capsule to over 3 million degrees Celsius and yielded 3.15 MJ of fusion energy output. The achievement was [announced earlier today](https://www.energy.gov/articles/doe-national-laboratory-makes-history-achieving-fusion-ignition) by officials and scientists representing the Department of Energy and its National Nuclear Security Administration, the White House, and LLNL during a [livestreamed event](https://youtu.be/Eke5PawU7rE).

## [One year later: Three peer-reviewed papers tell the story of NIF’s record yield shot](https://www.ans.org/news/article-4218/one-year-later-three-peerreviewed-papers-tell-the-story-of-nifs-record-yield-shot/)

![](/file/7075/Picture1.jpg)

Just over one year ago, on August 8, 2021, researchers achieved a yield of more than 1.3 megajoules (MJ) at [Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s](https://www.llnl.gov/) [National Ignition Facility](https://lasers.llnl.gov/) (NIF) for the first time, achieving scientific ignition and getting closer to fusion gain.

The scientific results of the historic experiment [were published](https://www.llnl.gov/news/three-peer-reviewed-papers-highlight-scientific-results-national-ignition-facility-record) exactly one year later in three peer-reviewed papers: one in [*Physical Review Letters*](https://journals.aps.org/prl/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevLett.129.075001) and two (an [experimental paper](https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.106.025202) and a [design paper](https://journals.aps.org/pre/abstract/10.1103/PhysRevE.106.025201)) in *Physical Review E*. In recognition of the many individuals who worked over decades to enable the ignition milestone, more than 1,000 authors are included on the *Physical Review Letters* paper.

## [Burning plasma state achieved at Lawrence Livermore Lab](https://www.ans.org/news/article-3613/burning-plasma-state-achieved-at-lawrence-livermore-lab/)

![](/file/5731/inertial confinement fusion designs.jpg)

One of the last remaining milestones in fusion research before attaining ignition and self-sustaining energy production is creating a burning plasma, where the fusion reactions themselves are the primary source of heating in the plasma. A paper published in the journal [*Nature*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41567-021-01485-9) on January 26 describes recent experiments at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory’s National Ignition Facility (NIF) that have achieved a burning plasma state.

## [National Ignition Facility experiment achieves record-breaking yield](https://www.ans.org/news/article-3169/national-ignition-facility-experiment-achieves-recordbreaking-yield/)

![](/file/4568/NIF target bay.png)

Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory is celebrating the yield from an experiment at the National Ignition Facility (NIF) of more than 1.3 megajoules of energy—eight times more than the yield from experiments conducted this spring and 25 times more than [NIF’s 2018 record yield](https://nam12.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Flink.mediaoutreach.meltwater.com%2Fls%2Fclick%3Fupn%3Da3HkpxmedX8HVUkNy8lZf6MeDlW8PjRqmeHGckWk-2F-2FPyuKx8NCZFdIyo8Nz-2BnmfCfoAqOspgLSDJxNmuZvOTBYU4aS8ZgKYffoS9C0G6xh8-3DvlhG_SFXrV84jPRgO2S2BD9xiK6z7bMm5a2lmUdT5iobQ8RwCw6TjaL83rSnwgONk49w3ug7FP1ZoB-2BuNLF2GpahpUqr-2B5joQMOy8KLu4HtTuv2X9pD-2B5CKJ2wJgwc0tgb0YIflxXipar2PLYJgfriS5n65a9UBcXsF9PoqAJLacz9pH-2FZz4UHcaxlCNxym3URkZhMnlJoy-2BMxSFfx0UIXOcK51kd5DgKGxcXK7i7S8b5jqA-2BrgVjTsilF4hD7AGgMwW9go9fXbruevvAPuFQsZC23AJAYOdAttNnWukMc5SkOYx-2BSKtHG2qrgD3M0o0bSk2YGvyz94DvxfrdUtVGOd3D-2BvLDXjDGuanptdxtnbb4CXW4LNTJca7esTxhnQTn5NRB&data=04%7C01%7Csgallier%40ans.org%7Cfd64f09016a44a50fd0208d9618fcccb%7C167ee8c474f046ce9d341192bc28f12d%7C0%7C0%7C637648092526300070%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C1000&sdata=O5Fm%2FcLM%2BNmrGoNyT5EBbGAO1fDPlQx%2BdjqP8Ag%2Bp8M%3D&reserved=0).

## [First Light fires first shots from gun built for pulsed fusion](https://www.ans.org/news/article-2901/first-light-fires-first-shots-from-gun-built-for-pulsed-fusion/)

![](/file/3886/Firstlight Fusion Gun 2x1.jpg)

Inside a new steel-clad facility nicknamed “The Citadel,” First Light Fusion has installed a 22-meter two-stage gas gun—the third-largest such component in Europe.

# Achieving Fusion Ignition

![NIF's Eighth Ignition Shot Sets Records](https://lasers.llnl.gov/sites/lasers/files/2025-07/ignition-banner.jpg "NIF's Eighth Ignition Shot Sets Records")

For the last six decades, LLNL researchers and their colleagues have been working to achieve one of the most challenging goals in all of science and a primary objective of NIF: fusion ignition. The goal [was reached](https://www.llnl.gov/news/national-ignition-facility-achieves-fusion-ignition) on Dec. 5, 2022, thanks to a wide-ranging international partnership and sustained efforts by every aspect of NIF and LLNL operations—the laser, targets, optics, diagnostics, modeling and simulations, and the people who make it all possible (see [“Keys to Our Success”](https://lasers.llnl.gov/about/keys-to-success)).

![animated Ignition image](https://lasers.llnl.gov/sites/lasers/files/2023-04/P15268183_WO19396_ignition_only_20_sec_opener_320x180_0.gif "animated Ignition image")

 Achieving ignition—producing more fusion energy than the amount of laser energy delivered to the NIF target—provides new opportunities for [stockpile stewardship](https://lasers.llnl.gov/science/nif-and-stockpile-stewardship "NIF and Stockpile Stewardship ") applications; NIF’s recreation of extreme environments allows scientists to study the behavior of materials under weapons-relevant conditions that were previously impossible to achieve in a laboratory setting. The 2022 achievement is not only vital for maintaining the effectiveness and safety of the U.S. nuclear arsenal but also enhances the prospects for an [inertial fusion energy](https://ife.llnl.gov/ "Inertial Fusion Energy") future.

In “indirect drive” inertial confinement fusion (ICF) experiments on NIF, up to 192 laser beams are fired into a centimeter-sized hollow cylinder called a hohlraum. This generates a “bath” of soft x rays that ablate, or blow off, the surface of a peppercorn-sized capsule suspended in the hohlraum.

The result is a rocket-like implosion that compresses and heats partially frozen hydrogen isotopes inside the capsule to conditions of pressure and temperature found only in the cores of stars and giant planets and in exploding nuclear weapons. The speed of the implosion—more than 400 kilometers per second—allows the fusion reactions to take place before the fuel can disassemble; the fuel is trapped by its own inertia (hence the term inertial confinement fusion).

![Image of a Fusion Reaction](https://lasers.llnl.gov/sites/lasers/files/styles/scaled_300w/public/2025-06/fusion-04.jpg?itok=TbUXoqPo "Image of a Fusion Reaction")

## What Is Fusion?

Fusion describes what happens when the nuclei of light atoms such as hydrogen overcome the repulsive electrostatic force that keeps them apart. When the nuclei get close enough, the force that binds protons and neutrons together, the strong force, takes over and pulls the nuclei even closer together so they “fuse” into a new, heavier helium nucleus with two neutrons and two protons.

The helium nucleus, also known as an alpha particle, has a slightly smaller mass than the sum of the masses of the two hydrogen nuclei, and the difference in mass is released as energy according to Albert Einstein’s famous formula E=mc2. The energy is released in the form of the alpha particles, high-energy neutrons, and other forms of energy such as electromagnetic radiation.

Nuclear fusion is different from nuclear fission, where the nuclei of heavy elements like uranium are split, forming two lighter elements—the process used in today’s nuclear power plants. In both nuclear reactions, the elements themselves change and become new elements—and in the process, a small amount of mass is converted to a large amount of energy.

## What Is Ignition?

![Illustration of a Rugby Hohlraum](https://lasers.llnl.gov/sites/lasers/files/styles/scaled_300w/public/2021-05/fusion-hohlraum.jpg?itok=k4nvc8lk "Illustration of a Rugby Hohlraum")

Fusion ignition occurs when the heating power from alpha particles produced by fusion reactions in the hot spot at the center of the target capsule overcomes the cooling effects of x-ray losses, electron conduction, and implosion expansion. When enough alpha particles are “stopped,” or absorbed, in the high-density fuel layer, a process known as alpha heating, a burn wave of fusion reactions propagates into the cold fuel surrounding the hot spot. When the energy deposition by the alpha particles contributes more than 50 percent of the heating of the fuel, a self-sustaining feedback loop known as a [“burning plasma”](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/nature-how-researchers-achieved-burning-plasma-regime-nif) results in an explosive amplification of energy output.

The NIF experiment on Dec. 5, 2022, far surpassed the ignition threshold by producing 3.15 megajoules (MJ) of fusion energy output from 2.05 MJ of laser energy delivered to the target. LLNL researchers continue to repeat the ignition achievement with increasing yield and target gain:

Additional experiments using higher laser energies and producing even higher energy yields are expected, further demonstrating that NIF can repeatedly conduct fusion experiments at multi-megajoule levels of energy output.

![NIF's Eighth Ignition Shot Sets Records](https://lasers.llnl.gov/sites/lasers/files/2025-07/ninth-ignition_0.jpg "NIF's Eighth Ignition Shot Sets Records")

Achieving ignition was an unprecedented, game-changing breakthrough that advances the science of ensuring the reliability of the nation’s nuclear stockpile (see [“NIF and Stockpile Stewardship”](https://lasers.llnl.gov/science/nif-and-stockpile-stewardship)) as well as the [potential use of fusion](https://lasers.llnl.gov/science/energy-security) as a safe, clean, and virtually unlimited energy source.

## Gaining New Understanding

While reaching ignition on NIF proved more challenging than first expected, researchers gained new understanding of the process with every experiment. For example, they learned important lessons about the initial limitations of their simulations and used that knowledge to continually improve both the models and implosion performance.

In particular, the advent of  [high-resolution 3D modeling and simulations](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/improving-3-d-models-could-help-guide-nifs-pursuit-ignition) contributed to a better understanding of the perturbation sources—including such “engineering features” as the thin membranes that suspend the target capsule inside the hohlraum and the fill tubes used to inject fuel into the capsule—that interfere with implosion performance. Other factors inhibiting energy yield are laser-plasma and  [hydrodynamic](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/improving-understanding-nif-implosion-instabilities) instabilities, asymmetries in the hohlraum x-ray flux that drives the implosion, and the mixing of capsule material with the fuel.

The addition of [new and enhanced diagnostics](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/nuclear-diagnostics-help-pave-way-ignition-on-nif), such as multiple line-of-sight neutron detectors, also led to improved target performance. The ability of new high-performance supercomputers and powerful  [“deep learning” techniques](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/deep-learning-surrogate-models-could-hasten-new-scientific-discoveries) to process, analyze, and simulate the mountain of data produced by these diagnostics in 3D over a broad range of perturbation sources—capsule surface imperfections, engineering features, drive asymmetries—significantly improved the ability of simulations to match, and [even predict](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/new-machine-learning-tactic-sharpens-nif-shot-predictions), experimental results (see [“Pursuing Ignition: A Decade of Progress”](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/building-to-a-solution-elements-of-a-fusion-breakthrough#decade)).

## Setting Energy Records

Since the first NIF laser shots, [scientists have set records](https://lasers.llnl.gov/about/keys-to-success/nif-sets-power-energy-records) for neutron yield and the amount of energy generated. Neutron yields have grown exponentially since NIF experiments began; more energy is being coupled to the target capsule; implosion velocities have increased; and the pressures in the center of the implosion are many times higher. The result has been a steady increase in energy yield that culminated with the NIF experiment that produced a yield of more than 8 MJ, more than six times the record yield of 1.35 MJ set in 2021 (see [“Threshold of Ignition”](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/video-threshold-ignition)).

NIF also made history in May, 2018, when it fired a record 2.15 MJ of ultraviolet energy to the Target Chamber—a 15 percent improvement over NIF’s design specification of 1.8 MJ and more than 10 percent higher than NIF’s previous 1.9 MJ energy record set in March, 2012. Subsequent experiments have further boosted the laser's energy to a new record high of 2.2 MJ.

Researchers are now poised to further improve NIF’s performance by coupling even more laser energy to the capsule while maintaining symmetry control and minimizing fuel contamination from target capsule material, or “mix.” We also are pursuing strategies like [novel hohlraum designs](https://lasers.llnl.gov/news/scientists-encouraged-initial-frustraum-experiments-nif); larger and thicker capsules; and new methods for finishing, mounting, and filling capsules.

We owe our progress to the decades of remarkable work by [previous generations of LLNL scientists and engineers](https://lasers.llnl.gov/about/keys-to-success/legacy-of-lasers) who advanced the field of high energy density and ICF science to where it is today. A new generation of LLNL researchers are carrying on this tradition of excellence.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Inertial Confinement Fusion (ICF) utilizes powerful lasers to compress and heat fusion fuel capsules, aiming for controlled nuclear fusion similar to processes in stars.
Magnetized Target Fusion combines aspects of magnetic confinement and inertial confinement, seeking to achieve fusion by compressing plasma with magnetic fields and physical compression.
Field-Reversed Configurations (FRC) create a compact plasma state with reversed magnetic fields, allowing potential improvements in stability and confinement for fusion reactions.
Alternative fusion methods may offer enhanced efficiency, reduced costs, and faster pathways to achieving practical fusion energy, alongside diverse technological applications.
Research trends focus on optimizing target designs, enhancing energy coupling, and exploring diverse fusion concepts, including advancements in computational modeling and experimental techniques.
